content.problemStatement:
Forgotten languages (also known as extinct languages) are languages that are no longer in use. Such languages were, probably, widely used before and no one could have ever imagined that they will become extinct at some point. Unfortunately, that is what happened to them. On the happy side of things, a language may be dead, but some of its words may continue to be used in other languages.



Using something called as the Internet, you have acquired a dictionary of **N** words of a forgotten language. Meanwhile, you also know **K** phrases used in modern languages. For each of the words of the forgotten language, your task is to determine whether the word is still in use in any of these **K** modern phrases or not.
constraints: Each word consists of only lowercase English letters and has length between 1 and 5.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and K – the number of forgotten‑language words and the number of modern phrases. The next line contains N space‑separated strings: the dictionary. Then K lines follow; each line begins with an integer L (the length of the phrase) followed by L space‑separated strings – the words of that phrase.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated tokens. The i‑th token is "YES" if the i‑th dictionary word appears in at least one of the K phrases, otherwise "NO".

sampleTestCases.input:
2
3 2
piygu ezyfo rzotm
1 piygu
6 tefwz tefwz piygu ezyfo tefwz piygu
4 1
kssdy tjzhy ljzym kegqz
4 kegqz kegqz kegqz vxvyj
sampleTestCases.output:
YES YES NO
YES NO NO YES
sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: dictionary words are piygu, ezyfo, rzotm. Both piygu and ezyfo appear in the two phrases, rzotm does not → YES YES NO. Test case 2: only kegqz appears in the single phrase → YES NO NO YES.
The only dictionary word "abc" is present in the phrase, therefore the answer is YES.
Neither "hello" nor "world" occurs in any of the two phrases, so both answers are NO.

hidden.label: maximum reasonable size
hidden.expectedOutput: YES YES YES YES YES YES YES YES YES YES YES YES YES YES YES

hidden.label: duplicate words in phrases
hidden.input:
1
3 2
x y z
3 x x x
2 y y

hidden.label: all words appear across multiple phrases
hidden.input:
1
4 3
ab cd ef gh
2 ab xy
3 cd ef gh
1 zz

hidden.label: no word appears
hidden.input:
1
5 2
one two three four five
3 a b c
2 d e

solution.approach: For each test case we store the N dictionary words. While reading the K phrases we insert every word of every phrase into a hash set (or simply compare on the fly because the limits are tiny). After all phrases are processed we iterate over the dictionary and check whether each word is present in the set. If it is, output "YES" otherwise "NO". The whole procedure is linear in the total number of words of the input.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total number of words in the test case) ≤ O(N + K·L) which is at most O(100 + 50·50) ≈ O(2600) per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + K·L) for the hash set of phrase words, at most a few thousand short strings.
